source_text: sources/comments/files/20251205-5303__California-Public-Utilities-Commission/FERC Docket NO. RM26-4-000 Reply Comments of the CPUC.txt
overall_summary: The CPUC argues the ANOPR exceeds FERC's jurisdiction under the FPA and that retail load energization is a state matter, supported by Commission precedent and FPA section 212(h). If the Commission proceeds, the CPUC urges flexible guidelines over fixed rules, cooperative federalism with deference to states, and transparency measures on queues and cost allocation. The CPUC also shifts its MW threshold position upward and supports non-MW factors to define the data center customer class.

quotes.text: the proposed rulemaking, as described in the ANOPR, would extend beyond FERC's jurisdiction and undermine the system of cooperative federalism set forth in the Federal Power Act and amendments in the Energy Policy Act of 2005 ("FPA").
quotes.concern: ANOPR exceeds FERC jurisdiction under the FPA

quotes.text: The Commission and the states have separate zones of exclusive jurisdiction.  Critically, and key to the analysis here, the states retain authority over matters not expressly granted to the Commission under the FPA.
quotes.concern: State authority over retail matters not expressly granted to FERC

quotes.text: This type of indirect impact is beyond the scope of the Commission's jurisdiction under the Federal Power Act.
quotes.concern: Indirect wholesale market effects from retail load do not establish FERC jurisdiction

quotes.text: FPA section 212(h) prohibits FERC from directing the provision of transmission directly to end users (retail wheeling).  The FPA reservation of jurisdiction over retail rates and services to the states includes retail load energization.
quotes.concern: FPA section 212(h) bars FERC from mandating retail transmission service

quotes.text: The terms of large load energization are not a sale of electric energy at wholesale and therefore fall into the "any other" category reserved for the states.
quotes.concern: Large load energization terms are retail, not wholesale, under 16 U.S.C. 824(b)

quotes.text: expansion in Federal jurisdiction would lead to confusion and likely conflict between federal regulation and state-approved tariffs.
quotes.concern: Federal jurisdictional expansion would conflict with state tariffs

quotes.text: FERC has consistently recognized, including in Order Nos. 2003 and 2023, that interconnection service is distinct from transmission service.  Yet under the ANOPR's reasoning, the act of connecting a large retail customer directly to transmission facilities could transform that retail delivery into a FERC-jurisdictional wholesale transaction – redefining distribution facilities and costs as transmission assets, shifting oversight of distribution expansion, maintenance, and rate recovery from state commissions to FERC.
quotes.concern: ANOPR risks reclassifying retail distribution as wholesale transmission

quotes.text: either this process would slow energization of large load and extend the timeline for bringing AI data centers online, or these questions would go unaddressed, resulting in unreasonable cost-shifting and a less reliable electric grid for all.
quotes.concern: Jurisdictional confusion will delay energization or cause cost-shifting

quotes.text: this ANOPR should be used to refine the proposals and prompts for a subsequent Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR"), providing sufficient time for input from utilities, grid operators, customers, and other stakeholder groups.
quotes.concern: ANOPR should feed a full NOPR, not rush to final action by April 2026

quotes.text: "Given the magnitude of the request and the importance of the issues implicated by that request, it would be patently unreasonable and bad public policy for FERC to use an expedited process to expand its jurisdiction."
quotes.concern: Expedited process for jurisdictional expansion is unreasonable

quotes.text: flexibility and deference to states within their domains would benefit ratepayers, utilities, and industry alike.  Otherwise, the ANOPR's efforts may be counterproductive to the mutual goal of promptly energizing AI data centers.
quotes.concern: State flexibility is necessary for timely large load energization

quotes.text: "developing voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency, requiring load interconnection processes that enhance transparency, and other novel approaches" necessary at a particular moment.
quotes.concern: Cooperative federalism through voluntary best practices with states

quotes.text: flexible guidelines would be more effective than cumbersome rules.
quotes.concern: Flexible guidelines preferred over prescriptive fixed rules

quotes.text: "Any standardized procedures dealing with large load interconnection, including any efforts to address the costs of studying these customers, must be flexible enough to account for the evolving understanding of just what is necessary to integrate these customers to the grid."
quotes.concern: Standardized study procedures must accommodate evolving grid integration knowledge

quotes.text: transparency measures "are necessary to protect residential consumers from bearing the costs of overbuilt transmission infrastructure resulting from inaccurate or inflated demand projections."
quotes.concern: Queue transparency protects ratepayers from inflated demand-driven overbuilds

quotes.text: the Commission should require public utilities, Regional Transmission Organizations and Independent System Operators to provide public posting of:  1. All interconnection requests above [the MW threshold], including project type and location;  2. Cost responsibility determinations and any resulting upgrades; and   3. Annual reporting on the consumer cost impacts of large-load interconnections, associated transmission expansions, and the impact on electricity and capacity markets rates for consumers other than large loads.
quotes.concern: Public posting of queue, cost responsibility, and consumer cost impact reports

quotes.text: Transparency in methodology for cost causation and cost allocation is critical to ensure the costs related to large load customers transmission network upgrades ("TNU's") are not unreasonably allocated in electricity rates to other customers.
quotes.concern: Cost causation methodology must be transparent to prevent cost-shifting to other ratepayers

quotes.text: the CPUC shifts its positions on the MW threshold towards a higher value and expands consideration of metrics for defining this large load customer class.
quotes.concern: CPUC revises position to support a higher MW threshold and non-MW class criteria

quotes.text: MW metrics alone may not be the defining feature of this type of load.  The CPUC agrees with the Ohio Office of Federal Energy Advocate ("Ohio FEA") that data centers are a unique customer class, and identifying them based on their exceptional characteristics is not unduly discriminatory.
quotes.concern: Data centers should be defined by characteristics beyond MW, not solely by size

quotes.text: if a final rule includes a nationwide MW threshold, the conservative next-best option is to set a threshold on the higher end of the spectrum of those proposed in Comments.
quotes.concern: If a nationwide MW threshold is set, it should be at the high end of proposed values

bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Exceeds FPA
bins.description: The CPUC argues the ANOPR exceeds FERC authority under the FPA. Retail load energization is not a wholesale transaction, FPA section 212(h) bars retail wheeling mandates, and Commission precedent reserves such matters to states. Expanding federal jurisdiction risks reclassifying distribution assets as transmission and forcing retail unbundling.

bins.name: Flexible Guidelines Over Fixed Rules
bins.description: If the Commission proceeds, the CPUC urges flexible guidelines rather than prescriptive rules. State and regional differences, evolving technology, and the diversity of large load customers mean that a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive and slow energization.

bins.name: Voluntary Best Practices for Studies
bins.description: The CPUC supports cooperative federalism through voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency and study procedures. Standardized study processes must remain flexible enough to evolve with grid integration needs.

bins.name: Cost Allocation Transparency
bins.description: The CPUC supports public posting of cost responsibility determinations, annual consumer cost impact reports, and transparent methodology for cost causation. Costs from large load transmission upgrades should not be shifted to other ratepayers without clear, standardized criteria.

bins.name: Higher MW Threshold With Non-MW Criteria
bins.description: The CPUC shifts its position toward a higher MW threshold in response to Initial Comments showing broad variation across regions. It also supports defining the data center customer class by characteristics beyond MW alone, to protect local manufacturers and enable fair retail cost allocation.

bins.name: Regulatory Stability for Investment
bins.description: Perceived regulatory instability reduces investment. The CPUC warns that jurisdictional conflict between new federal rules and existing state-approved tariffs would undermine investment conditions and slow grid buildout.

bins.name: Slower Process, Full NOPR Needed
bins.description: The CPUC opposes rushing to final action by April 2026 and argues the ANOPR should lead to a full NOPR with adequate comment periods. An expedited process for a jurisdiction-expanding rule is unreasonable under 42 U.S.C. 7173(b) and the APA.

bins.name: Public Queue Posting Requirements
bins.description: The CPUC supports public posting of all large load interconnection requests, cost responsibility determinations, and annual consumer cost impact reports. Queue transparency prevents load shopping, queue gaming, and inflated demand projections that inflate wholesale rates and transmission costs.

bins.name: Cooperative Federalism With States
bins.description: The CPUC urges the Commission to build on state progress in large load energization through cooperative federalism rather than federal mandates. Voluntary best practices and deference to state regulatory frameworks will advance timely, reliable, and affordable energization.
